ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'fish soup' class (FOODON_03311737); no species-specific snakehead fish soup entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC represents fish soup as a category. No snakehead-specific code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for snakehead fish soup or a sufficiently close fish soup preparation. Null is appropriate.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of snakehead fish soup served in a Singaporean hawker or restaurant context weighs approximately 400 g, comprising broth, fish slices, and optional vegetables or tofu.

nutrient_source.title: Fish soup (with fish slices) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists 'fish soup with fish slices' as the closest approved match for snakehead fish soup. Snakehead (Channa striata) is a lean white fish with a nutritional profile closely comparable to the generic fish soup entry in HPB. Values are adapted from HPB fish soup data. Protein content is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for snakehead fish (raw), which reports approximately 18–19 g protein per 100 g raw fish; the soup dilution yields approximately 7–9 g per 100 g soup. The HPB fish soup entry is used as the primary reference for the composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — fish-based soups and broths
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for snakehead fish soup. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al., 2021, Am J Clin Nutr) report GI values for clear fish-based soups and broth-based dishes in the range of 25–35, consistent with low-carbohydrate, high-protein soups. A GI of 30 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on the low carbohydrate content (1.8 g per 100 g) and the high protein and water content of the dish. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (30 × 7.2 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.2, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 400 g serving provides approximately 1280 mg sodium, which represents over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size and avoid adding soy sauce or fish sauce at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 30, snakehead fish soup has a negligible glycemic load and is a suitable high-protein, low-GI meal option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein source with low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Snakehead fish is a lean protein source with low saturated fat (0.4 g per 100 g) and provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids that support cardiovascular health; however, the sodium content of the broth warrants attention for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Snakehead Fish Soup (生鱼汤, Sheng Yu Tang) is a traditional Chinese-Singaporean clear broth soup made from snakehead fish (Channa striata), known locally as haruan. The dish is prepared by simmering fresh snakehead fish slices in a light broth typically seasoned with ginger, tomato, preserved vegetables, white pepper, and salt. The result is a clean, mildly flavoured soup with tender, firm white fish slices. The broth is clear to slightly milky depending on preparation. Snakehead fish is prized in Singapore and Southeast Asia for its high-quality lean protein content, low fat profile, and traditional belief in its wound-healing and anti-inflammatory properties, making it popular as a post-surgery or convalescent food. Nutritionally, the soup provides approximately 52 kcal per 100 g, with 7.8 g protein, 1.5 g fat, and only 1.8 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-calorie, low-carbohydrate dish. The glycemic index is estimated at 30 (low), and the glycemic load per serving is very low at approximately 2.2. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic impact, and for those seeking lean protein sources for muscle repair and recovery. Sodium content from the broth is the primary concern for hypertensive individuals. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at seafood soup hawker stalls and zi char restaurants, often accompanied by steamed white rice or rice vermicelli.
common_user_queries: Is fish soup good for diabetics?
Is snakehead fish soup low carb?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists specifically for snakehead fish soup as a named dish. Values are adapted from the HPB fish soup (with fish slices) category, which is the closest approved primary source match. Snakehead fish nutritional data from USDA FoodData Central (raw Channa striata) is consistent with the adapted values. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fish soup category data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo 'fish soup with fish slices' as the closest approved primary source; no exact snakehead-specific composite dish entry was found in HPB, USDA, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases at the time of processing. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB fish soup source entry. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB fish soup source entry. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB fish soup source entry. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed as effectively zero for a minimally processed fish soup with no hydrogenated fats. 6. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database fish-based soup category; no direct GI measurement for snakehead fish soup was found in peer-reviewed literature. 7. The input record described this as 'All Fish Soup' without the signature meatball; this variant is reflected in the alias list. 8. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and other micronutrients are estimated from USDA FoodData Central snakehead fish data and HPB fish soup data combined. 9. Sodium content is a key clinical concern for this dish due to the broth base; restaurant preparations may vary significantly. 10. The dish is served at Yan Ji Gourmet Seafood Soup (Funan) per the input record; this is a single-restaurant observation and does not affect nutrient values.
